title: The Return Statement 📤
description: Get results back out of your methods.

slides.title: The Outbox 📤
slides.content:
Parameters act like an Inbox, taking data in.

But what if we don't want the method to just `println()` the result? What if we want the method to calculate a value and **give it back** so we can use it elsewhere?

We need an Outbox. We use the **return** keyword.

slides.title: What does 'void' mean? 🕳️
slides.content:
## The Void

Remember `static void greetPlayer()`? 

The word **`void`** literally means "empty space". It tells Java: "This method will do some stuff, but it will NOT give any data back."

quizData.question: If a method signature includes the word `void`, what does it imply?

options.text: It does not return any value back to where it was called.

quizData.explanation: Void means 'no return'. The method just executes its code block and finishes.

slides.title: Replacing 'void' ✨
slides.content:
If we want our method to pass back an integer, we replace `void` with `int`.

Then, inside the method, we must use the `return` keyword to spit out the final integer.

quizData.question: If you write a method that takes a Player's ID and fetches their Username, what should replace `void` in the method signature?
quizData.explanation: Since a Username is a word, the method will be pushing out text. So the return type must be `String`.

slides.title: Return is a Break! 🛑
slides.content:
## Important Rule

The moment Java hits a `return` statement, it instantly escapes the method and brings the data back.

Any code written *below* a `return` statement inside that block will never execute!
